```{r}
text(pop_mean, 18, expression(mu), cex = 1.3)
text(2411, -3, expression(bar(X)), cex = 1.3)
# CI brackets
segments(2411 - 2*se, 5, 2411 + 2*se, 5, lwd = 2)
segments(2411 - 2*se, 3, 2411 - 2*se, 7, lwd = 2)
segments(2411 + 2*se, 3, 2411 + 2*se, 7, lwd = 2)
text(3200, 35, "Therefore, lines extending\n2 standard errors outward\nfrom the sample estimate\nwill bracket the true parameter\nin roughly 95% of samples",
     cex = 0.85)
```

### Code

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

set.seed(42)
pop_data <- rgamma(100000, shape = 2.5, rate = 0.001)
pop_data <- pop_data[pop_data <= 15000]
pop_mean <- mean(pop_data)
sample_means <- replicate(10000, mean(sample(pop_data, 100)))
se <- sd(sample_means)

par(mfrow = c(2, 2), mar = c(5, 4, 3, 1))

# Top-left: sampling distribution + SE arrow
hist(sample_means, breaks = 40, col = "#CC6633", border = "#CC6633",
     main = "", xlim = c(1500, 4000),
     xlab = expression("Sample mean length " * bar(X) * " (nucleotides)"),
     ylab = "Probability", freq = FALSE, las = 1)
arrows(pop_mean - se, 0.012, pop_mean + se, 0.012,
       code = 3, length = 0.1, lwd = 2)

# Top-right: sampling distribution + 2SE bracket
hist(sample_means, breaks = 40, col = "#CC6633", border = "#CC6633",
     main = "", xlim = c(1500, 4000),
     xlab = expression("Sample mean length " * bar(X) * " (nucleotides)"),
     ylab = "Probability", freq = FALSE, las = 1)
arrows(pop_mean - 2*se, 0.012, pop_mean + 2*se, 0.012,
       code = 3, length = 0.1, lwd = 2)

# Bottom panels: single sample with CI brackets
segments(2411 - 2*se, 5, 2411 + 2*se, 5, lwd = 2)
```

### Interpretation

-   The four panels progressively build the concept of standard error and confidence intervals
-   The standard error (top-left) is the standard deviation of the sampling distribution, measuring typical distance between a sample estimate and the true parameter
-   Extending 2 standard errors from a sample mean (bottom-right) creates a 95% confidence interval that brackets the true parameter in roughly 95% of samples
:::


## Standard Error of the Mean

::: panel-tabset
### Equation

$$\huge \sigma_{\bar{x}} \approx s_{\bar{x}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### LaTeX

``` text
\huge \sigma_{\bar{x}} \approx s_{\bar{x}} = \frac{s}{\sqrt{n}}
```

### Interpretation

-   The standard error of the mean equals the sample standard deviation divided by the square root of the sample size
-   SEM shrinks with larger samples because the square root of n is in the denominator
-   SEM estimates how much the sample mean would vary across repeated samples from the same population
:::

::: callout-note
-   SEM is NOT the standard deviation of the original distribution
-   SEM decreases as sample size increases
:::

## Calculating SEM

::: panel-tabset
### Output

```{r}
#| label: sem-calc-output
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

set.seed(32)
true_pop <- rnorm(n = 1000, mean = 2, sd = 5)

# Small sample
samps_5 <- replicate(n = 50, sample(true_pop, size = 5))
sem_5 <- sd(apply(samps_5, 2, mean)) / sqrt(50)

# Larger sample
samps_50 <- replicate(n = 50, sample(true_pop, size = 50))
sem_50 <- sd(apply(samps_50, 2, mean)) / sqrt(50)

cat("SEM (n=5):", round(sem_5, 4), "\nSEM (n=50):", round(sem_50, 4))
```

### Code

```{r}
#| label: sem-calc-code
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

set.seed(32)
true_pop <- rnorm(n = 1000, mean = 2, sd = 5)

# Small sample
samps_5 <- replicate(n = 50, sample(true_pop, size = 5))
sem_5 <- sd(apply(samps_5, 2, mean)) / sqrt(50)

# Larger sample
samps_50 <- replicate(n = 50, sample(true_pop, size = 50))
sem_50 <- sd(apply(samps_50, 2, mean)) / sqrt(50)

cat("SEM (n=5):", round(sem_5, 4), "\nSEM (n=50):", round(sem_50, 4))
```

### Interpretation

-   The standard error of the mean (SEM) quantifies uncertainty in the sample mean as an estimate of the population mean
-   SEM decreases with larger sample sizes, reflecting greater precision with more data
-   Comparing SEM at n=5 vs n=50 shows how increasing sample size dramatically reduces estimation uncertainty
:::

## Interactive: How Sample Size Affects the Estimate and SEM {.smaller}

Use the slider to change the sample size and observe how the **sampling distribution**, **estimate**, and **SEM** change. Each time, 500 samples of size *n* are drawn from a skewed population.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

viewof sampleSize = Inputs.range([2, 200], {
  value: 10,
  step: 1,
  label: "Sample size (n):"
})
```

```{ojs}
//| echo: false

// Seeded random number generator for reproducibility
function mulberry32(a) {
  return function() {
    a |= 0; a = a + 0x6D2B79F5 | 0;
    var t = Math.imul(a ^ a >>> 15, 1 | a);
    t = t + Math.imul(t ^ t >>> 7, 61 | t) ^ t;
    return ((t ^ t >>> 14) >>> 0) / 4294967296;
  }
}

// Generate exponential random variable using inverse CDF
function randExp(rng, rate) {
  return -Math.log(1 - rng()) / rate;
}

// Generate population (exponential with rate=0.5, mean=2)
{
  const rng = mulberry32(12345);
  const popSize = 10000;
  const population = Array.from({length: popSize}, () => randExp(rng, 0.5));

  const trueMean = population.reduce((a, b) => a + b) / population.length;

  // Draw 500 samples of size n and compute means
  const nSamples = 500;
  const n = sampleSize;
  const sampleMeans = [];

  const sampleRng = mulberry32(42);
  for (let i = 0; i < nSamples; i++) {
    let sum = 0;
    for (let j = 0; j < n; j++) {
      const idx = Math.floor(sampleRng() * popSize);
      sum += population[idx];
    }
    sampleMeans.push(sum / n);
  }

  const grandMean = sampleMeans.reduce((a, b) => a + b) / sampleMeans.length;
  const variance = sampleMeans.reduce((a, b) => a + (b - grandMean) ** 2, 0) / (sampleMeans.length - 1);
  const sdMeans = Math.sqrt(variance);
  const sem = sdMeans / Math.sqrt(nSamples);
  const theoreticalSE = Math.sqrt(4) / Math.sqrt(n); // SD of Exp(0.5) = 1/rate = 2

  // Create histogram data
  const nBins = 35;
  const minVal = Math.min(...sampleMeans);
  const maxVal = Math.max(...sampleMeans);
  const padding = (maxVal - minVal) * 0.1;
  const binWidth = (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding) / nBins;
  const bins = Array.from({length: nBins}, (_, i) => {
    const lo = minVal - padding + i * binWidth;
    const hi = lo + binWidth;
    const count = sampleMeans.filter(v => v >= lo && v < hi).length;
    return {lo, hi, mid: (lo + hi) / 2, count};
  });

  const maxCount = Math.max(...bins.map(b => b.count));

  return html`
    <div style="display: flex; gap: 24px; align-items: flex-start; font-family: system-ui, sans-serif;">
      <div style="flex: 1.3;">
        <svg viewBox="0 0 500 300" style="width: 100%; max-width: 500px;">
          <!-- Axes -->
          <line x1="60" y1="250" x2="480" y2="250" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>
          <line x1="60" y1="250" x2="60" y2="20" stroke="#333" stroke-width="1.5"/>

          <!-- Title -->
          <text x="270" y="16" text-anchor="middle" font-size="14" font-weight="bold" fill="#333">
            Distribution of Sample Means (n = ${n})
          </text>

          <!-- Bars -->
          ${bins.map(b => {
            const barH = maxCount > 0 ? (b.count / maxCount) * 210 : 0;
            const x = 60 + ((b.lo - (minVal - padding)) / (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding)) * 420;
            const w = (binWidth / (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding)) * 420;
            return svg`<rect x="${x}" y="${250 - barH}" width="${w - 1}" height="${barH}"
                          fill="#3498DB" opacity="0.85" stroke="white" stroke-width="0.5"/>`;
          })}

          <!-- Grand mean line -->
          ${(() => {
            const x = 60 + ((grandMean - (minVal - padding)) / (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding)) * 420;
            return svg`<line x1="${x}" y1="250" x2="${x}" y2="25" stroke="#E74C3C" stroke-width="2.5"/>`;
          })()}

          <!-- True mean line -->
          ${(() => {
            const x = 60 + ((trueMean - (minVal - padding)) / (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding)) * 420;
            return svg`<line x1="${x}" y1="250" x2="${x}" y2="25" stroke="#F39C12" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,4"/>`;
          })()}

          <!-- SEM bracket -->
          ${(() => {
            const cx = 60 + ((grandMean - (minVal - padding)) / (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding)) * 420;
            const left = 60 + ((grandMean - sdMeans - (minVal - padding)) / (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding)) * 420;
            const right = 60 + ((grandMean + sdMeans - (minVal - padding)) / (maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding)) * 420;
            const y = 32;
            return svg`
              <line x1="${left}" y1="${y}" x2="${right}" y2="${y}" stroke="#2ECC71" stroke-width="2.5"/>
              <line x1="${left}" y1="${y-5}" x2="${left}" y2="${y+5}" stroke="#2ECC71" stroke-width="2"/>
              <line x1="${right}" y1="${y-5}" x2="${right}" y2="${y+5}" stroke="#2ECC71" stroke-width="2"/>
              <text x="${cx}" y="${y - 8}" text-anchor="middle" font-size="11" font-weight="bold" fill="#2ECC71">
                SD of means = ${sdMeans.toFixed(3)}
              </text>
            `;
          })()}

          <!-- X-axis labels -->
          ${(() => {
            const range = maxVal - minVal + 2 * padding;
            const step = range / 5;
            return Array.from({length: 6}, (_, i) => {
              const val = minVal - padding + i * step;
              const x = 60 + (i / 5) * 420;
              return svg`<text x="${x}" y="268" text-anchor="middle" font-size="10" fill="#666">${val.toFixed(1)}</text>`;
            });
          })()}
          <text x="270" y="290" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#333">Sample Mean</text>
          <text x="25" y="140" text-anchor="middle" font-size="12" fill="#333" transform="rotate(-90,25,140)">Count</text>
        </svg>
      </div>

      <div style="flex: 0.7; padding-top: 20px;">
        <div style="background: #f8f9fa; border-radius: 10px; padding: 18px; border: 1px solid #dee2e6;">
          <div style="font-size: 14px; margin-bottom: 12px;">
            <span style="display:inline-block; width:12px; height:3px; background:#E74C3C; margin-right:6px; vertical-align:middle;"></span>
            <strong>Estimate (x̄):</strong> ${grandMean.toFixed(3)}
          </div>
          <div style="font-size: 14px; margin-bottom: 12px;">
            <span style="display:inline-block; width:12px; height:3px; background:#F39C12; margin-right:6px; vertical-align:middle; border-top: 2px dashed #F39C12;"></span>
            <strong>True μ:</strong> ${trueMean.toFixed(3)}
          </div>
          <div style="font-size: 14px; margin-bottom: 12px;">
            <span style="display:inline-block; width:12px; height:3px; background:#2ECC71; margin-right:6px; vertical-align:middle;"></span>
            <strong>SD of means:</strong> ${sdMeans.toFixed(3)}
          </div>
          <div style="font-size: 14px; margin-bottom: 16px;">
            <strong>Theoretical SE:</strong> σ/√n = ${theoreticalSE.toFixed(3)}
          </div>
          <hr style="border: none; border-top: 1px solid #dee2e6; margin: 12px 0;">
          <div style="font-size: 13px; color: #555;">
            <strong>Key insight:</strong><br>
            As <em>n</em> increases, the distribution narrows (SE ∝ 1/√n) and becomes more normal — regardless of population shape.
          </div>
        </div>
      </div>
    </div>
  `;
}
```

## Confidence Intervals

A confidence interval is a range of values about a parameter estimate such that we are X% certain the true population parameter lies within.

::: panel-tabset
### Output

```{r}
#| label: conf-int-output
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

# 95% CI using t.test
sample_data <- rnorm(30, mean = 10, sd = 2)
t.test(sample_data)$conf.int
```

### Code

```{r}
#| label: conf-int-code
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

# 95% CI using t.test
sample_data <- rnorm(30, mean = 10, sd = 2)
t.test(sample_data)$conf.int
```

### Interpretation

-   The `t.test()` function returns a 95% confidence interval for the population mean
-   The interval provides a range of plausible values for the true parameter based on the sample data
-   Wider intervals indicate greater uncertainty; narrower intervals indicate more precise estimates
:::

## Visualizing What "95% Confidence" Means

::: panel-tabset
### Output

```{r}
#| label: fig-ci-demo-code
#| fig-cap: "95% of CIs from repeated sampling contain the true mean (μ = 10)"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5
#| echo: false
#| eval: true

true_mean <- 10
n_samples <- 50

# Take 50 samples and calculate CIs
results <- t(replicate(n_samples, {
  samp <- rnorm(20, mean = true_mean, sd = 2)
  ci <- t.test(samp)$conf.int
  c(mean = mean(samp), lower = ci[1], upper = ci[2])
}))

# Plot CIs - color red if they miss the true mean
contains_true <- results[, "lower"] <= true_mean & results[, "upper"] >= true_mean
colors <- ifelse(contains_true, "steelblue", "red")

plot(NULL, xlim = c(8, 12), ylim = c(0, n_samples + 1),
     xlab = "Value", ylab = "Sample Number", main = "50 Confidence Intervals")
abline(v = true_mean, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)
for(i in 1:n_samples) {
  segments(results[i, "lower"], i, results[i, "upper"], i, col = colors[i], lwd = 2)
  points(results[i, "mean"], i, pch = 19, cex = 0.6, col = colors[i])
}
legend("topright", c(paste(sum(contains_true), "contain μ"),
       paste(sum(!contains_true), "miss μ")), col = c("steelblue", "red"), lwd = 2)
```

### Code

```{r}
#| label: fig-ci-demo-figure
#| fig-cap: "95% of CIs from repeated sampling contain the true mean (μ = 10)"
#| fig-width: 9
#| fig-height: 5
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

true_mean <- 10
n_samples <- 50

# Take 50 samples and calculate CIs
results <- t(replicate(n_samples, {
  samp <- rnorm(20, mean = true_mean, sd = 2)
  ci <- t.test(samp)$conf.int
  c(mean = mean(samp), lower = ci[1], upper = ci[2])
}))

# Plot CIs - color red if they miss the true mean
contains_true <- results[, "lower"] <= true_mean & results[, "upper"] >= true_mean
colors <- ifelse(contains_true, "steelblue", "red")

plot(NULL, xlim = c(8, 12), ylim = c(0, n_samples + 1),
     xlab = "Value", ylab = "Sample Number", main = "50 Confidence Intervals")
abline(v = true_mean, col = "darkgreen", lwd = 2)
for(i in 1:n_samples) {
  segments(results[i, "lower"], i, results[i, "upper"], i, col = colors[i], lwd = 2)
  points(results[i, "mean"], i, pch = 19, cex = 0.6, col = colors[i])
}
legend("topright", c(paste(sum(contains_true), "contain μ"),
       paste(sum(!contains_true), "miss μ")), col = c("steelblue", "red"), lwd = 2)
```

### Interpretation

-   Each horizontal line represents a 95% confidence interval from a different random sample
-   Blue intervals contain the true population mean; red intervals miss it
-   Approximately 95% of intervals capture the true mean, illustrating the frequentist interpretation of confidence
-   This demonstrates that "95% confidence" refers to the long-run coverage rate, not the probability that any single interval contains the parameter

### Try It

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| eval: false

# Try changing the confidence level to 99% and see how interval width changes
true_mean <- 10
n_samples <- 50

results_99 <- t(replicate(n_samples, {
  samp <- rnorm(20, mean = true_mean, sd = 2)
  ci <- t.test(samp, conf.level = 0.99)$conf.int
  c(mean = mean(samp), lower = ci[1], upper = ci[2])
}))

# How does the coverage change? Are the intervals wider or narrower?
contains_true <- results_99[, "lower"] <= true_mean & results_99[, "upper"] >= true_mean
cat("Coverage at 99%:", sum(contains_true), "/", n_samples)

# Also try: What happens with smaller samples (n=5) vs larger (n=100)?
```
:::

## Coefficient of Variation

To compare standard deviations across populations with different means:

::: panel-tabset
### Equation

$$CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%$$

### LaTeX

``` text
CV = \frac{s}{\bar{x}} \times 100\%
```

### Interpretation

-   The CV expresses the standard deviation as a percentage of the mean
-   This allows comparison of variability between populations with very different means
-   A higher CV indicates greater relative variability
:::
